content:
AUDIENCE:
The reader is a curious 8-year-old child who enjoys learning about how the world works, especially topics related to health and nature. They probably know that germs can make people sick and that doctors give medicine called antibiotics to help fight these germs. They care about staying healthy and might be worried if medicines stop working. They might get confused by big scientific words or very detailed explanations, and they could get bored by anything too long or too complicated. A friendly, simple, and fun tone with easy examples and comparisons will help keep them interested and make the idea clear.

===BEGIN===
Have you ever taken medicine when you were sick? Some medicines, called antibiotics, help fight tiny bugs called bacteria that make us feel bad. But sometimes, these bacteria learn how to fight back against the medicine! This is called antibiotic resistance.

Imagine if you played a game where the bad guys keep changing their costumes so you can’t catch them. That’s like bacteria changing to stay safe from antibiotics. This happens mostly because people sometimes use antibiotics too much or not the right way, like stopping medicine too early or using it when they don’t need it.

When bacteria become resistant, the medicine doesn’t work well anymore, and it’s harder to get better. That’s why doctors say we should only use antibiotics when we really need them and always finish the medicine, so the bacteria don’t get stronger. This helps keep everyone healthy!
===END===
